coredata.dc:title: An innovative immunosensor for ultrasensitive detection of breast cancer specific carbohydrate (CA 15-3) in unprocessed human plasma and MCF-7 breast cancer cell lysates using gold nanospear electrochemically assembled onto thiolated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Tagi, Solmaz
Solhi, Elham
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Shadjou, Nasrin
Eftekhari, Aziz
Mahboob, Soltanali
coredata.dc:description: The accurate quantification of the level of breast cancer specific protein CA 15-3 in serum is crucial for cancer prognosis. This work, a novel and sensitive label-free immunoassay based on gold nanospear (Au NSs) electrochemically assembled onto thiolated graphene quantum dots (CysA/GQDs) for the detection of CA 15-3 antibodies. The CysA/Au NSs/GQDs hybrid interface provides a large surface area for the effective immobilization of CA 15-3 antigens, as well as it ascertains the bioactivity and stability of immobilized CA 15-3 antigens. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), and EDS photoelectron spectroscopies were used to monitor the sensor fabrication. Also, cyclic voltammetry was used to quantify the extent of Au NSs' surface coverage by CA 15-3 antigens. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) was employed to investigate the immunosensor fabrication and to monitor the binding events between CA 15–3 antigens-antibodies. Under optimized experimental conditions, the immunosensor displayed good sensitivity and specificity. The CA 15-3 were detected in a concentration as low as 0.11U/mL with a linear range from 0.16–125U/mL. The high sensitivity of the immunosensor may derive from the high loading of CA 15-3 antibodies on CysA/Au NSs/GQDs hybrid interface which increases the number of binding events. The method was successfully applied assay of the CA 15-3 in unprocessed human plasma samples. Also, proposed immunosensor was applied to the assay of CA 15-3 malignant cell line lysates (human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF-7).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Immunoassay
Biomarker
Gold nanoparticles
CA 15-3 tumor protein
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesize and cytotoxicity study of GQDs
Fabrication of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs on the surface of GCE
The SEM, TEM, AFM, and EDS characterization of the modified sensing interface
Immunosensor selectivity and real sample testing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GQDs induced cell death in (A) HepG2 cells and (B) non-tumorigenic cell line (NIH-3T3). Cells were treated with (0,10, 20, 50, 100 and 200ppm GQDs for 24, 48 and 72h and cell viability was measured using MTT [3(4,5dimethylthiazol2yl) 2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide] reduction assay. Data represent the mean±S.E.M. (n =6). Fluorescence images of Hela cells: (C) control cells, (D & E) cells treated with 200ppm of GQDs for 24h.

A) Electrochemical deposition of GQDs on the surface of GCE. The cyclic voltammogram depicts grasignal growth of GQDs after 30 successive cycles in the range of 0 to +1V with scan rate of 0.1V/s. B) Chronoamperogram for the electrochemical deposition of AuNPs on the surface of CysA/GQDs/GCE. The pplied potential E=0V vs. Ag/AgCl and the record time was 500s.

FE-SEM (A&B) and TEM (C) images of the GCE (A) and GQDs/GCE (B&C). D) FE-SEM images of the CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. E) FE-SEM images of the CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs GCE. F) FE-SEM images of the CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE in different magnification.
EDX spectra of (A) (GQDs/GCE, B) CysA/GQDs/GCE, C) CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE/GCE, D) CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE, and E) CA 15-3/BSA/Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE/GCE.

A) SWVs of (GQDS/GCE, CysA/GQDs/GCE, CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE, CA 15–3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE, and CA 15-3/BSA modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE in 0.01M [Fe(CN)6] 3−/4-, KCl. SWV parameters: potential range of 0V to 0.8V with a step potential of 5mV, amplitude 20mV and frequency 25Hz. B) Histogram of the peak currents obtained by SWV versus different steps of electrode preparation).

A) SWVs of CA 15-3/BSA/Anti- CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE in different concentrations of CA 15-3 (0.16, 0.20, 0.25, 0.33, 0.40, 0.50, 0.60, 1.00, 2.00, 15.00, 50.00, and 125.00U/mL). Supporting electrolyte is 0.01M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, KCl. SWV parameters: potential range of 0V to 0.8V with a step potential of 5mV, amplitude 20mV and frequency 25Hz. (B) Calibration curves of the CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE.
The schematic illustration of the label free electrochemical immunosensor for detection of CA 15-3 protein.
A comparison of the performance of several immunosensor for the detection of CA15-3.

head.ce:title: An innovative immunosensor for ultrasensitive detection of breast cancer specific carbohydrate (CA 15-3) in unprocessed human plasma and MCF-7 breast cancer cell lysates using gold nanospear electrochemically assembled onto thiolated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, Uremia University, Uremia 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Basic Sciences, Maragheh University of Medical Sciences, Maragheh, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: g
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Toxicology Research Center, Maragheh University of Medical Sciences, Maragheh, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate quantification of the level of breast cancer specific protein CA 15-3 in serum is crucial for cancer prognosis. This work, a novel and sensitive label-free immunoassay based on gold nanospear (Au NSs) electrochemically assembled onto thiolated graphene quantum dots (CysA/GQDs) for the detection of CA 15-3 antibodies. The CysA/Au NSs/GQDs hybrid interface provides a large surface area for the effective immobilization of CA 15-3 antigens, as well as it ascertains the bioactivity and stability of immobilized CA 15-3 antigens. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), and EDS photoelectron spectroscopies were used to monitor the sensor fabrication. Also, cyclic voltammetry was used to quantify the extent of Au NSs' surface coverage by CA 15-3 antigens. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) was employed to investigate the immunosensor fabrication and to monitor the binding events between CA 15–3 antigens-antibodies. Under optimized experimental conditions, the immunosensor displayed good sensitivity and specificity. The CA 15-3 were detected in a concentration as low as 0.11U/mL with a linear range from 0.16–125U/mL. The high sensitivity of the immunosensor may derive from the high loading of CA 15-3 antibodies on CysA/Au NSs/GQDs hybrid interface which increases the number of binding events. The method was successfully applied assay of the CA 15-3 in unprocessed human plasma samples. Also, proposed immunosensor was applied to the assay of CA 15-3 malignant cell line lysates (human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF-7).

ce:section.ce:para: The incidence of breast cancer has been increasing over the years. To control and monitor of this disease several tumor biomarkers have been proposed for early diagnosis, patient follow-up and/or treatment guidance. The development of screening programs and improvements of treatment and follow-up have been able to lower the mortality in the past few years, however it is still the leading cause of cancer-related mortality in women [1]. Therefore, several challenges still remain, including diagnosis, definition of high risk recurrence population, early diagnosis of recurrence and guidance through the different lines of treatment in the management of metastatic disease [2]. Important research has been conducted to identify suitable biomarkers of breast cancer, which has led to advances in its detection and treatment [3]. These biomarkers can be one of several substances that are produced by cancer cells, and their levels can be monitored in tumor tissues or biological fluids such as blood, urine, serum, plasma, stool, sputum or nipple discharge. Many breast cancer biomarkers have been suggested, but only CA 15-3 has been routinely adopted as a potential predictor of treatment failure in the metastatic setting. A decrease or return to normal levels of this biomarker may indicate that the cancer has responded favorably to therapy [4].

Carbohydrate antigen 15-3 (CA15-3) is a tumor marker that plays an important role in the diagnosis and screening for breast cancer. The serum CA15-3 level is below 30U/mL for healthy individuals. Also, when the concentration of CA15-3 is over 100U/mL, the patients can be considered to suffer from breast cancer [5]. The concentration of CA15-3 provides valuable information for monitoring the therapy. It can also early detection of recurrence of breast cancer after curative surgery [6]. On such a basis, much attention has been devoted to developing procedures to detect the presence of CA 15-3 protein at very low concentrations in a physiological environment and biological fluids. Some analytical methods, like chemiluminescence immunoassay [7], optical immunoassay [8], enzyme-linked immunosorption assay [9], and sandwich-type immunosensor [10], have been developed for detection of CA 15-3. However, these methods require complicated operations, expensive and complex equipment, and time-consuming pretreatment steps. Accordingly, electrochemical immunosensor has the advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, low cost and specific recognition and therefore, have been widely applied in the detection of tumor markers [11–19]. Among the above successful examples, label-free immunosensor has drawn significant attention, which is based on the inhibiting the direct electron transfer generated from the redox probe to the electrode.

As the interaction of antibody with antigen is insufficient to produce a sensitive electrochemical test signal directly, great progress is alternative by using advanced nanomaterial as amplification elements. Graphene-based nanocomposites have been used in various applications including electrochemical biosensing of anlytes [20–23]. In some of research works, these nanocomposites combine the intrinsic properties of Au nanoparticles (NPs) with those of graphene-based materials, such as high electrical conductivity and enhanced surface area, which are essential for the high sensitivity of biosensors. Owing to their intrinsic properties, GQDs and gold nanoparticles could form an ideal platform for the CA 15-3 protein immobilization and sensitive assay of CA 15-3 antibodies.

Various approaches have been used for the preparation of graphene-based materials/Au NPs modified surfaces. These include, the simultaneous formation of reduced graphene oxide and Au NSs by electrodeposition, [24] layer-by-layer assembly of reduced graphene oxide and Au NPs, [25,26] electrodeposition of Au NSs onto the graphene modified surfaces, [27] drop casting of graphene followed by electrodeposition of AuNPs, [28] and drop casting of graphene oxide/AuNPs suspensions [29].

Electrodeposition synthesis of nanostructured materials (including gold nanostructures) is a potentially superior method due to advantages of having a high degree of controllability, being single-step process and easy control, having effective controllable of size and shape of the electrodeposits, being easy to anchor securely on the substrate, producing uniform and high pure deposits, being environmentally friend, and providing more opportunities for the design and fabrication of different devices [30–34].

Based on this phenomena, the present study propose a novel platform for the sensitive and selective detection of the CA 15-3 antibody. In the present study, one-step, green, and template-less electrodeposition methods were developed to fabricate gold nanostructures with nanoscale sizes and suitable shapes. Gold nanoparticles (Au NSs) electrochemically assembled onto thiolated graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The fully electrochemical fabrication method has been proposed for preparation of GQDs modified surfaces to overcome the drawbacks of other methods such as uncontrolled thickness and inhomogeneity of the layer. The terminal carboxylic groups of the GQDs were then activated through carbodiimide chemistry for GQDs thiolation with cysteamine (CysA). Subsequently, the thiolated GQDs was used to assemble Au NSs via AuS bonds (CysA/Au NSs/GQDs). The CA 15-3 protein was immobilized onto the Au NSs through the interactions of the positively charged CA 15-3 amine groups and the nanocomposite (CysA/Au NSs/GQDs). The sensor modification was monitored by surface characterization and electrochemical techniques such as Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), EDS, cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV). The drop in the SWV peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was attributed to the response of the CA 15-3 antibody binding to the sensor. This biosensor has the potential to be used as a tool for early breast cancer diagnosis, by integrating it with other conventional tests.

Human CA 15-3 ELISA kit (CanAg/CA15-3 EIA) including CA 15-3 antigen, anti-CA 15-3 standard, biotinilated solution and avidin-biotin-peroxidase complex (ABC), standard buffer and dilute solutions were obtained from CanAg Diagonestic AB Technology Co., (Gotenberg, Sweden). Potassium ferrocyanide K4Fe(CN)6, potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6, disodium hydrogen orthophosphate, potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate potassium chloride, sodium nitrite, hydrochloric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride(EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), cysteamine hydrochloride, hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III)hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.05M) was prepared by Na2HPO4 (0.1M) and NaH2PO4 (0.1M) with 0.15M of NaCl in deionized water. Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Electrochemical measurements were carried out in a conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm) powered by an electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT 302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using NOVA 1.7 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10mV and the equilibrium time was 5s. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (from Azar Electrode Co., Urumia, Iran). For square-wave voltammetry (SWV) measurements, a amplitude time=0.02s, stop potential=0.001V, frequency=10Hz and a scan rate of 0.05Vs−1 were employed. All the electroanalytical tests were carried out at 25°C in 0.01M [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3kV, and chemical compositions of the electrod were analysed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment. TEM (transmission electron microscopy) analysis was conducted on a Philips CM30 electron microscope operated at 200kV (Adelaide, Australia). Atomic force microscopic (AFM) analysis was carried out by Nanosurf (AG Gräubernstrasse 124410 Liestal Switzerland) in a tapping mode to confirm the surface immobilization of aptamers on the substrates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize and cytotoxicity study of GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: GQDs was synthesized and characterized according to our previous report [35]. An easy bottom-up method was used for the preparation of GQDs. At first, GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing citric acid and dispersing the carbonized products into alkaline solutions. Briefly, 2g of citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle until the citric acid changed to an orange liquid. Then, for preparing GQDs, 100mL of 10mg/mL NaOH solution was added into the orange homogenous liquid dropwise with continuous stirring. The obtained GQD solution was stable for at least one month at 4°C.

To determine the effects of GQDs-induced toxicity NIH-3T3 and HepG2 cells were exposed to different concentrations of GQD (0, 10, 20, 50,100 and 200ppm) for 24, 48 and 72h. Fig. 1 represent the impact of GQDs treatment on the viability of cancerous cell line (HepG2) (A) and non-tumorigenic cell line (NIH-3T3) (B). Assessment of cell viability by MTT reduction cell viability test revealed that the increasing doses of graphene quantum dot up to 200ppm did not show any significant effects on cell death of both cell lines.

Also, the average lateral dimension of as-prepared GQDs was quite small compared with HeLa cells. It has been reported that nano-sized GQDs could be internalized into cells faster compared with the micro-sized GO sheets. Moreover, as-prepared GQDs emitted intense green luminescence, so it might be a good candidate for fluorescent probes in cell imaging. As shown in Fig. 1 (C–D), the laser scanning confocal microscopy image of the cells incubated (Fig. 5b and c) with 200ppm of GQDs was obviously brighter than that of the cells without GQDs treatment (Fig. 1a), demonstrating that GQDs had internalized into the cells and mainly existed in cell membrane and cytoplasm region, in agreement with the previous literature reported by Yuan's group. The results revealed that even at the low concentration of 50ppm, the fluorescence of the cells was still bright, which held great potential for bioimaging. In addition, GQDs could enter the cell cytoplasm, indicating another prospect of GQDs in drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs on the surface of GCE
ce:section.ce:para: For this purpose, GQDs modified GCE (GQDs/GCE) was prepared and characterized according to our previous works [36]. Fig. 2A shows the electrochemical deposition of GQDs on the surface of GCE. The GQDs/GCE were further modified with thiol (–SH) groups by binding the COOH groups of GQDs to the amine (NH2) groups of CysA through amide bonds. The SH modified electrode (CysA/GQDs/GCE) was used thereafter for electrochemical assembly of Au NSs. For this purpose, a fully electrochemical method for preparation of the substrate were used (Fig. 2B). The CysA/GQDs/GCE was placed in the solution contained 100mM (HAuCl4). Electrodeposition was performed at 0mV for 500s. Then CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE electrode was then rinsed thoroughly with distilled water.

The CA 15-3 antibody immunosensor were assembled by immobilizing the CA 15-3 protein (10μl of 1U/mL) onto the CysA/GQDs/Au NSs/GCE through electrostatic interactions between the CA 15-3 amine groups and the Au NSs in 5h at 4°C. Following CA 15-3 incubation, the electrodes were thoroughly rinsed with wash buffer (0.1% Tween-PBS (pH8.5)) to remove the unbound CA 15-3 protein. Then BSA solution (3%) was added to the modified surface and left for 1h to block the free sites of CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE surfaces to minimize the non-specific binding. The immunosensor was then directly subjected to immunochemical reaction with CA 15-3-Ab or to electrochemical measurements. For CA 15-3 antibody sensing, 10μL of CA 15-3-Ab were dropped onto the sensor surface and left for 1h. Prior to electrochemical measurements, the immunosensor were washed thoroughly with wash buffer. Scheme 1 summarizes the fabrication steps of the immunosensor.

For activation of the –COOH groups of antibodies, EDC/NHS chemistry was used where EDC (0.2M) worked as a coupling agent and NHS (0.05M) as an activator. A solution mixture of anti-CA 15-3, EDC and NHS in 2:1:1 ratio was made and kept at room temperature (25°C) for 30min. Further, 10μL of activated antibody solution was uniformly spread over CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE and kept in a humid chamber at room temperature (25°C) during the next 1h. Thereafter, it was washed with PBS to remove any unbound antibody molecules. BSA (0.1M) was used for blocking the non-specific active sites. The fabricated CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/GQDs/AuNPs/GCE immunoelectrode was washed with PBS and stored at 4°C until further use. The immunosensor were then directly subjected to immunochemical reaction with CA 15-3 Ab or to electrochemical measurements.
For every test, all of modification steps should be performed. In this case, all of electrodes were prepared as fresh sensor before electrochemical evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM, TEM, AFM, and EDS characterization of the modified sensing interface
ce:section.ce:para:
For investigation of surface morphology FE-SEM was used. Figs. 3–6
shows the FE-SEM images of GCE, GQDs/GCE, CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE, CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE and CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. As can be seen, the surface of unmodified GCE (Fig. 3A) is smooth although the FE-SEM image of the GQDs (Fig. 3B) displays a number of aggregated bright dots positioned over the entire surface particularly at the wrinkle sites. The results were confirmed by TEM images of GQDs on the surface of GCE (Fig. 3C).

The FE-SEM images of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE shows spherical particles with uniform structure (Fig. 3D), revealing that the Au NSs were indeed assembled onto the surface of thiolated GQDs. Interestingly, more dispersed and less aggregated Au NSs are seen upon the electrostatic interactions of CysA/GQDs with the AuNPs. Possibly the binding of positively charged CysA/GQDs to the negatively charged AuNPs reduced the aggregation of the NPs through steric hindrance, although the loss of some AuNPs from the GQDS surface cannot be eliminated.
FE-SEM images recorded at high magnifications reveal that the spear like gold nanolayers are comprised from ensembles of nanoparticles of 50nm.

Interestingly, after incubation of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE with CA 15-3 protein, the morphology of electrode was changed. It's found the, a binding between CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE and protein was occurred (Fig. 3E). Therefore, CA 15-3 was successfully immobilized on the surface of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. More importantly, incubation of CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE with specific antibody (Anti-CA 15-3 Ab) lead to new change on the morphology of electrode. As can be seen, the Anti-CA 15-3 Ab was linked to CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE using Ag-Ab immunocomplex (Fig. 3F). These results confirmed the successful assembly of proposed immunosensor.

As evident in Fig. 4, signals for C, and O elements belong to both GQDs and CysA, while N and S is only attributed to CysA. The large and sharp Au peak is the typical signal for bulk gold element. The corresponding EDX spectra of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE (Fig. 4) demonstrates the increase in Au peak, which clearly indicates the electrodeposition of nano-size gold layer.

AFM imaging was performed in dry state which is a cheaper and more basic alternative compared to AFM imaging in liquid environment. AFM images of Au electrode after modification by CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE seem to have significant differences in morphology (Fig. S1 (see supplementary data)) with uniform roughness presented in corresponding histograms. Therefore modification significantly changes those properties of the GCE surface in applied resolution scale. After attachment of CA 15-3 protein, the differences in morphology and roughness (Fig. S2 (see supplementary data)) of substrate (CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE) were detectable at AFM equipment resolution scale. From the image presented it clearly seems that 80–140nm diameter and 20nm height features appeared on the surface of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. It is estimated that observed features are CA 15-3 protein and/or its complexes covalently immobilized on the surface of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. The results obtained illustrate that success of covalent protein immobilization can be estimated by AFM. The AFM images (Fig. S3, (see supplementary data)) registered after 1h incubation CA 15-3/BSA modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE in solution containing anti-CA 15-3 antibodies showed significant differences in morphology and roughness (Fig. S3, see supplementary data)) if compared with and morphology registered before this treatment or obtained by equally treated CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE surface which was not covered by CA 15-3 protein (Fig. S3 (see Supplementary data)). In images of mica surface investigated by AFM after exposure of CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE to anti-CA 15-3 Ab containing solution two distinct populations of features with height-radius corresponding to those of CA 15-3 and antigen-antibody complex are observed. It allowed to distinguish between immobilized antigens and antigen-antibody complexes formed. The formed features allow us to conclude that we observe the antigen-antibody docking reaction, or the appropriate lack thereof, between single antigen molecules immobilized on the surface and single or multiple antibody molecules. After 1h treatment of CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE modified with CA 15-3/anti-CA 15-3 Ab complex in the complement protein containing solution, sparse 500nm diameter and almost 400nm height structures appeared on the surface. Appearance of significantly higher features was confirmed by appearance of significantly higher structures in corresponding histogram. We believe that those structures were based on complement system proteins self-assembled on the basis of Ag-Ab complexes, while no such structures were detected if just CA 15-3 modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE or unmodified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE was treated by the same complement system protein containing solution for the same period.

The immunosensor fabrication steps were also monitored by recording SWVs from an equimolar solution of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4-. SWV of the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution at the CysA/GQDs/GCE surface showed a slightly lower peak potential (from 0.056V to 0.019V for GQDs electrode) and a higher peak current (Fig. 5). The possible reason could be the electrostatic attraction between the terminal SH and the anionic redox probes [37]. After the assembly of Au NSs onto the CysA/GQDs/GCE surface, the peak potential shifted to −0.034V and the peak current decreased. Upon CA 15-3 protein binding onto the Au NP surface, the peak current further decreased due to the steric effect of the bulky CA 15-3 protein. Finally, further decrease of the Ip was observed following the BSA blocking of the CA 15-3/GQDs immunosensor surface by covering the remaining exposed Au NSs and GQDs surfaces.

The detection process of the CA 15–3 antibody is shown in Scheme 1. The CA 15–3 protein layer attached to the CysA/Au NSs/GQDs was used to recognize CA 15–3 antibodies. The drop in the SWV oxidation peak current (Ip) of the [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− couple upon CA 15–3 Ab recognition by the immunosensor surface was used as immunosensor. An optimization study was performed prior to immunosensor application, since the immunosensor's performance might be affected by the CA 15–3 protein concentration of the CA 15–3 antibody and the time to incubate it onto the sensor's surface. The CA 15–3 concentration was varied to determine the maximum quantity of the CA 15–3 protein to be attached to the CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE. Fig. 6 A&B shows the response of the blocked immunosensors at different CA 15–3 protein concentrations for 1U/mL CA 15–3 antibody solution. The response which represents the percentage change in the SWV peak current before and after interaction with the CA 15–3 antibody, was used for evaluation. According to obtained results, the coverage of the Au NSs varies with the concentration of CA 15–3 in the solution. Thus, 10μL of 1U/mL from CA 15–3 protein were used as the optimum CA 15–3 concentration for successive experiments.

The incubation time to bind the CA 15–3 antibody was also optimized. The immunosensors' response at various incubation times for CA 15–3 antibody was recorded. The response value (peak current) increases by increasing the incubation time from 10 to 60min, after which it levels off. Finally, the optimal incubation time for further investigation was chosen to be 1h.
2.2.
CA 15–3 antibody immunosensor

Fig. 6 (A and B) shows that increasing concentration of the CA 15–3 antibody from 0 to 125U/mL and 0.16–125U/mL has led to systematically lower peak current values. The calibration plot was then constructed by plotting the peak current against the Neperian logarithm of CA 15–3 antibody concentration (Fig. 6C). A wide dynamic range was found between 0 and 125U/mL. The reproducibility expressed in terms of the relative standard deviation (RSD) was about 5.11% (n =4). The limit of detection (LOD) was determined to be 0.011U/mL (signal-to-noise ratio of 3). This LOD is lower than that of conventional ELISA of (1U/mL). The results of the immunosensor are comparable with other sensors that were displayed in Table 1 . This confirms that the presented immunosensor shows better analytical performances to than other techniques [4,6,11,45–47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunosensor selectivity and real sample testing
ce:section.ce:para: Control experiments were performed in the presence of some coexistent proteins as interferences to evaluate the immunosensor selectivity. For this purpose, SWV experiments were carried out to check the cross-reactivity of the CA 15-3 (15U/mL) with possible coexisting and interfering compounds, such as BSA, p53, PSA, and HSA. The prepared immunosensor (CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE) was incubated with interfering compounds in the supporting electrolyte. The peak current produced by the CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE is almost similar (Fig. S4 (see supplementary data)). The successive addition of higher concentrations of interfering compounds did not make any appreciable increment in peak current, shows that the proposed sensor exhibits remarkable selectivity towards CA 15-3. This can be attributed to the suitable specificity of the CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE immunosensor to detection of CA 15-3.

The immunosensor was tested for the detection of CA 15-3 Ab in human plasma samples (Fig. S5 (see supplementary data)). Also, the recoveries calculated from the calibration plot established in the buffer were in the range of 91.2 to 101.9% with a RSD of 1.9% to 10.3%, indicating acceptable accuracy and demonstrating that the immunosensor has the potential for detecting CA 15-3 antibodies in real samples.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, we investigated the performance of the developed electrochemical immunosensor in quantitative analysis of cancer cell lysates. The peak currents of the proposed immunosensor were linearly proportional to the MCF-7 cells concentration. The sensitivity of the developed immunosensor was investigated by varying the concentration of MCF-7 cells lysates with the sensitive SWV detection. As shown in Fig. S6 (see supplementary data) the oxidation peak current increased gradually with the increase in the MCF-7 cell lysates concentration. The average oxidation peak current had a good linear relationship versus the concentration of MCF-7 cell in the range from 0.02–0.55cells·mL−1 with a low limit of quantification of 0.02cellsmL−1, which was comparable to those reported by most of assays and other electrochemical and fluorescence cytosensing strategy for cancer cell detection [38–45]. The linear regression equation was calculated as I (μA)=23.501 C(MCF-7) −3.4118, R2 =0.9727.

Stability of the proposed immunosensor was tested by measuring the decrease in voltammetric current during repetitive DPV measurements of with CA 15-3/BSA modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE stored in −4°C. Obtained results show that this immunosensor has not remarkable stability without significant change in the voltammetric currents. When the electrode was stored in the −4°C, the current response remained almost unchanged for about one day. The stability of the CA 15-3/BSA/Anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE in the terms of peak current/potential was also studied by storing the electrode at 4°C for nine days. It was found that the electrode is not stable in various days and the signal was not retained, which indicates that the sensor can offer application of this immunosensor in the prepared day (Fig. S7 (see supplementary data)).

To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording SWVs in buffer solution, containing 10ng/mL of CA 15-3. The relative standard deviation was 5.11%. Therefore, CA 15-3/BSA/anti-CA 15-3 Ab modified CysA/Au NSs/GQDs/GCE imparts a suitable stability onto measurements of CA 15-3 by SWV techniques.

We have successfully designed a new platform based on Au NSs and GQDs for sensitive voltammetric detection of CA 15-3. GQD film were prepared by the full electrochemically method onto the surface of glassy carbon electrodes. Au NSs were covalently bonded to thiolated GQD films and used to bind of CA 15-3 protein. The novel detection scheme is a label-free based immunoassay, which makes the protein detection simple and rapid. The immunosensor showed a linear dynamic range and a detection limit of 0.16–125U/mL and 0.11U/mL, respectively. The proposed immunosensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detection of CA 15-3 with high selectivity and reproducibility. The immunosensor detected CA 15-3 from spiked human plasma sample with high recovery values. We conclude that this platform has the potential to be used in clinical applications and point-of-care diagnosis of breast cancer a simple and rapid assay.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the partial financial support by the National Institute for Medical Research Development (NIMAD) for financial supporting of this research. Also, we thank Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for instrumental supporting of this research.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2018.03.183.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mater. Sci. Eng. C Mater. Biol. Appl.
